coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot fluorescent probes for food safety detection: Progress, opportunities and challenges
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wenshuo
Zhong, Hongda
Zhao, Pingping
Shen, Aiguo
Li, Houbin
Liu, Xinghai
coredata.dc:description: As a new photoluminescent nanomaterial, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have great potential in the detection of food ingredients and contaminants, owing to its merits of ease in preparation, good biocompatibility, adjustable modification, and various fluorescence response methods. However, since the rules of CDs in detecting different substances are rarely summarized by existing reports, the design of probes is blind in most cases. In this review, CDs are introduced, and the synthesis methods and optical properties of CDs are discussed. Fluorescence response mechanism of CDs in the detection of metal ions, drugs, food additives, bacteria, and nutrients has been analyzed and compared. As a result, design methods of CDs probes are divided into three, and introduction of functional groups on the surface of CDs was confirmed to be the core of design. Intermolecular interactions that different target substances tend to occur are summarized, which determines the choice of carbon source and synthesis method. Advantages, weaknesses and development trends of CDs fluorescence detection in food safety are discussed.

Food safety

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot fluorescent probes for food safety detection: Progress, opportunities and challenges

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a new photoluminescent nanomaterial, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have great potential in the detection of food ingredients and contaminants, owing to its merits of ease in preparation, good biocompatibility, adjustable modification, and various fluorescence response methods. However, since the rules of CDs in detecting different substances are rarely summarized by existing reports, the design of probes is blind in most cases. In this review, CDs are introduced, and the synthesis methods and optical properties of CDs are discussed. Fluorescence response mechanism of CDs in the detection of metal ions, drugs, food additives, bacteria, and nutrients has been analyzed and compared. As a result, design methods of CDs probes are divided into three, and introduction of functional groups on the surface of CDs was confirmed to be the core of design. Intermolecular interactions that different target substances tend to occur are summarized, which determines the choice of carbon source and synthesis method. Advantages, weaknesses and development trends of CDs fluorescence detection in food safety are discussed.

Graphic showing a brief summary of the table of content covered in this review.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety is a persistent issue worldwide (Liu, Liu, Gao, & Zhang, 2015; Neethirajan, Ragavan, Weng, & Chand, 2018). More than 200 diseases caused by the ingestion of contaminated food or water have been identified by WHO, resulting in nearly two million deaths each year (Shrivastava, Shrivastava, & Ramasamy, 2015). Residues of harmful substances in food are the main threat to human health (Hoehl, Lu, Sims, & Slocum, 2012), including metal ion, pesticide and veterinary drug(Shi et al., 2019), illegal additives (Lei et al., 2016), pathogenic microorganisms (Yousefi, Ali, Su, Filipe, & Didar, 2018) and toxic industrial waste (Lam, Remais, Fung, Xu, & Sun, 2013). Therefore, Convenient and fast detection is necessary since contaminants may appear in any part of food processing.

CDs are a class of photoluminescence (PL) carbon nanomaterials with ultrafine sizes below 10 nm (Molaei, 2019a). The fluorescence of CDs is typically emitted under ultraviolet light (UV) excitation, and can be quenched or enhanced in the presence of specific substances (Devi, Saini, & Kim, 2019; Ji, Zhou, Leblanc, & Peng, 2020). Since the fluorescence intensity is linearly related to the concentration of the quencher within a certain range (Sharma et al., 2016; Zhang, Chen, et al., 2018), CDs are identified as potential detection probes (Li, Chen, Gooding, & Liu, 2019). Due to advantages such as abundant raw materials (Purbia & Paria, 2016), simple synthesis method (Roy, Preeyanka, Majhi, Seth, & Sarkar, 2018), biocompatibility (Lin, Li, Dong, Fu, & Chen, 2017) and rapid detection process with intuitive results (Thulasi, Kathiravan, & Asha Jhonsi, 2020), CDs are especially promising in food detection.

Although most food detection can already be achieved through traditional methods, such as gas chromatography (Zou et al., 2016), liquid chromatography (Uclés Moreno et al., 2014), electric signal sensor and surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (Alsammarraie & Lin, 2017). Although more accurate results can be obtained by these methods, they are usually time-consuming, demanding of samples, and reliant on large equipment. Therefore, in the scene of rapid detection, fluorescent probes are more valuable. Among the common fluorescent probes, metal fluorescent particles usually made of precious metals as raw materials are expensive (Goswami et al., 2016). Organic fluorescent polymers are limited by their own toxicity (Li, Su, et al., 2020). Compared with other inorganic nanodots, carbon-based CDs have the advantages of a wide range of raw materials, simple synthesis, stable properties, and diverse response modes (Xu, Lu, Li, & Yang, 2018a). Therefore, it is feasible to determine the edible value of food by detecting its harmful components and nutrients with CDs probe (Rodriguez et al., 2021).

A large number of methods to synthetic CDs have been reported (Dager, Baliyan, Kurosu, Maekawa, & Tachibana, 2020). The fluorescence performance of CDs can be improved by doping elements such as nitrogen (Zhu et al., 2013), boron (Dong et al., 2013), phosphorus (Reckmeier, Schneider, Susha, & Rogach, 2016), and sulfur (Barman, Jana, Bhattacharyya, & Patra, 2014). Furthermore, CDs can be mixed with metal ions or other nanoparticles (Wang, Li, Lu, et al., 2018) to detect specific substances.

This work is to provide valuable information for the design of CDs fluorescent probes used in food safety. There have been some reviews about the fluorescence detection (Qu, Wei, & Sun, 2018), but most of them are only a simple list of detectable substances (Dong, Cai, You, & Chi, 2015; Xiao & Sun, 2018), without analyzing the relationship between the types of detected substances and the fluorescence variation patterns of CDs. In this review, the synthesis methods, physicochemical properties and fluorescence mechanism of CDs are overlooked. And the fluorescence response modes when detecting different substances are summarized in order to summarize the design ideas of CDs probes. The current challenges and opportunities of CDs in food detection are pointed out.

In 2004, nanoparticles with fluorescence effect were discovered in the electrophoresis purification experiment of carbon nanotubes (Xu et al., 2004), which was officially named as “carbon quantum dots” in 2006 (Sun et al., 2006). All dotted nanomaterials mainly composed of carbon can be called CDs, including polymer dots (PD), carbon nanodots (CND) and graphene quantum dots (GQD) (Anwar et al., 2019). CDs are mainly composed of sp2 or sp3 hybridized conjugated carbon atoms as the nucleus and organic functional groups or polymer aggregates (Zhi, Yao, Cui, Orr, & Haynes, 2019). Under the excitation of light with specific wavelength, CDs will emit fluorescence (Yan et al., 2019). When specific substances are added, the fluorescence will be quenched, restored, enhanced or discolored (Li, Chen, & Xing, 2017), which makes CDs have great potential as fluorescent probes.

There are many materials can be used to synthesize CDs, including natural organic substances (Algarra et al., 2019; Mehta, Jha, Basu, Singhal, & Kailasa, 2015), organic extracts (Guo et al., 2019; Yuan et al., 2018) and some carbon-containing inorganic substances (Miao et al., 2018; Ye et al., 2013). The preparation of CDs with natural substances has received tremendous attention in current research (Liu, Chen, Li, & Huang, 2019).

CDs have good stability and low toxicity (Shen et al., 2019). In aqueous solution, the fluorescence of CDs can remain stable for a long time (Bayati, Dai, Zambrana, Rees, & Fidalgo De Cortalezzi, 2018, Li, Zhou, et al., 2018). The safety of CDs to the human body has also been confirmed by many experiments (Hu et al., 2017), which is helpful for its application in food detection.
The synthetic routes of CDs can be divided into Top-down and Bottom-up (Hu, 2014), which can be summarized as in Fig. 1 .

Top-down refers to the methods to obtain CDs by decomposing large size carbon sources (e.g., carbon black (Dong et al., 2012), graphene (Shtepliuk et al., 2017), soot (Tian et al., 2009), carbon fibers (Peng et al., 2012), etc.), including arc discharge (Xu et al., 2004), laser ablation (Li et al., 2011), electrochemical oxidation (Tan et al., 2015), and chemical oxidation (Lu et al., 2020), etc.

Bottom-up refers to the synthesis of CDs with small organic matter. Heat treatment (Deb, Konwar, & Chowdhury, 2020), hydrothermal/solvothermal (Miao et al., 2018), ultrasonic oscillation (Dang, Huang, Zhang, Wang, & Chen, 2016), microwave irradiation (Vallan et al., 2018) are typically bottom-up. The carbon sources used in Bottom-up can be small organic molecules (Li et al., 2020; Mura et al., 2020; Peng, Ji, Zhou, Zhao, & Leblanc, 2020; Peng, Zhou, et al., 2020), biomass materials (Liu et al., 2021; Qiu et al., 2020) and artificial compounds (González-González et al., 2020; Lauria & Lizundia, 2020). Compared with Top-down, Bottom-up is cheaper, simpler and more widely applied.

Hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment are the most widely used methods to synthesize CDs (Schneider, Bärtsch, Stark, & Grass, 2019), which usually dissolve the precursor in water or organic solvent and then make it decomposed, reacted or carbonized by heating. Hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis is simple and suitable for various carbon sources. The surface state and elemental composition of CDs can be easily adjusted through adding different precursors into the synthesis system (Wu, Zhao, et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2012). However, there are still some problems such as uneven size distribution, long reaction time and low QY (Park et al., 2014).

Photoluminescence is the basis for the application of CDs in food inspection (Molaei, 2020). In addition to basic quenching and enhancement, the fluorescence of some CDs can change color with the adjustment of excitation wavelength (Frank et al., 2020). Some CDs have up-conversion fluorescence ability (L. Jiang, Huang, et al., 2020; Ortega Liebana et al., 2019), which refers to the emission of high-energy fluorescence under the irradiation of low-energy excitation wavelength (Li et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2015; Zhu et al., 2015). However, the mechanism of fluorescence emergence still remains debatable. Theories currently supported mainly include band gap fluorescence theory and molecular fluorescence (Ehrat et al., 2017; Nguyen, Srivastava, Pan, & Gruebele, 2020; Wei, Yin, et al., 2020).

The band gap between π electrons of sp2 hybrid and π* excited state is one of the reasons affect fluorescence emission of CDs (Sutanto et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2015). This explains the influence of quantum size on fluorescence, which means the larger the size of CDs, the lower the band gap energy of π electron transition to excited state, along with the red shift of fluorescence emission peak. In a research, a series of CDs were synthesized with citric acid and diaminonaphthalene. When the quantum size increases from 1.95 nm to 6.68 nm, the band gap energy of electronic transition decreases from 3.02 eV to 2.12 eV (Yuan et al., 2017). Band-gap transition theory also explains the mechanism of fluorescent adjustable CDs. The fluorescent colors of CDs could be different with the change of solvent (Zhan et al., 2018). By adding cyanuric acid (CA) to CDs, the fluorescence of the CDs@CA complex changed from blue to green (Zhou, Ushakova, et al., 2020). This is because the band gap energy of the composite is reduced, which makes the fluorescence red shift.

The surface of CDs will have defects due to oxidation, which makes it easy for to capture excitons, resulting in different absorption/emission peaks (Cao, Meziani, Sahu, & Sun, 2012). In a report, eight kinds of CDs with an average particle size of 2.6 nm were synthesized (Ding, Yu, Wei, & Xiong, 2015). It is found that the more oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface, the more obvious the red shift. This is because the surface defects reduce the band gap, resulting in a red shift of fluorescence (Fig. 2 ).

According to fluorescence molecular theory, fluorescence is caused by small molecules or luminophores in CDs, which are generally formed during the process of bottom-up method (Essner, Kist, Polo-Parada, & Baker, 2018). In the initial stage of carbonization, small fluorescent particles due to the dehydration condensation between or within molecules. They can be either free in solution or connected to the interior or surface of carbon skeleton, depending on the synthesis temperature (Fig. 3 )(Schneider et al., 2017; Shen et al., 2018).

Generally, molecular fluorescence has higher intensity but lower stability than band gap fluorescence (Yan et al., 2019). The interaction of these two kinds of fluorescence can further improve the fluorescence mechanism, and has a great influence on the color development of CDs (Hu, Su, Lin, Chang, & Tung, 2018).

Most CDs can keep stable in solution, colloid or solid under common conditions (Zou et al., 2017). It is reported that the CDs synthesized with ammonium citrate can keep fluorescence stable in high concentration salt solution (Yang et al., 2014). Highly fluorescent graphene oxide/CDs solloid have been synthesized from rice precursor using a ball-milling-assisted hydrothermal method, which proves the stability of CDs in colloids. In addition, there are also studies about obtaining detection probes by adsorbing carbon quantum dots on colloids. For solids, it is more common to obtain fluorescent films by mixing CDs. In another research, the CDs was continuously irradiated with UV for 60 min, and the fluorescence intensity was decreased by only 8%. After 20 weeks of storage at −4 °C, its fluorescence intensity decreased by only 10% (Rahmani & Ghaemy, 2019).

Most CDs have no adverse effects on proliferation of cancer cells, physiological activities and organ functions of mice (Yang et al., 2009). In another report, CDs synthesized with soot is proven to have no genotoxicity at different concentration either (Wang et al., 2013). Low toxicity means that the application of CDs is safe and will not cause potential harm to operators (Lin et al., 2017).

Fluorescence of CDs can be quenched, enhanced or restored (Fig. 4 ). The linear relationship between the degree of fluorescence change and the concentration of the detected object in a certain range is the main basis for the quantitative detection with CDs (Molaei, 2019b).

Fluorescence quenching refers to all phenomena that lead to the decrease of fluorescence intensity and lifetime, which mainly include static quenching, dynamic quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photo-induced electron transfer (PET) inner filter effect (IFE) and aggregation fluorescence quenching (ACQ) (Zu et al., 2017).

Static quenching occurs when CDs can interact with quencher to form a stable non-fluorescent ground state complex. The compound can absorb light and immediately return to ground state without emitting photons, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence. Static quenching has three characteristics as follows (Zu et al., 2017).
(a)
Formation of ground state complex will lead to the change of absorption spectrum.
(b)
Fluorescence lifetime of CDs will not change.
(c)
Increase of temperature will decrease the stability of complex, thus reducing the effect of static quenching.

A typical progress is the reaction of Fe3+ with nitrogen and phosphorus doped CDs (N/P-CDs) (Shangguan et al., 2017). After Fe3+ was added, fluorescence of N/P-CDs at 320 nm decreased (Fig. 5 a). The zeta potential changed distinctly, indicating complex was produced (Fig. 5b). Fluorescence lifetime of N/P-CDs changed from 5.87 ns to 5.85 ns, which was almost negligible (Fig. 5c). Quenching effect of Fe3+ on N, P-CDs decreased when the temperature rose from 298 K to 318 K (Fig. 5d), which met the three criteria above.

Dynamic quenching is also known as collision quenching. CDs collides with quencher molecules in the excited state to produce energy transfer or electron transfer, thus returning to the ground state in the form of no radiation, resulting in a decrease of overall fluorescence intensity. Dynamic quenching has the following characteristics (Zu et al., 2017).
(a)
Dynamic quenching only affects the excited state of CDs, and thus it does not change the absorption spectrum of CDs.
(b)
In the presence of quencher, lifetime of CDs will change.
(c)
Dynamic quenching is directly related to the intensity of molecular activity. The increase of temperature will enhance the dynamic quenching effect, while the increase of viscosity will limit the molecular movement.

A process of CDs detecting water in organic solvents demonstrates the principle of dynamic quenching (Wei et al., 2018). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs has not changed before and after the introduction of water (Fig. 6 a), but its fluorescence lifetime has obviously declined from 8.565 ns to 7.905 ns (Fig. 6b), which is a typical dynamic quenching.

FRET refers to the quenching when the emission spectrum of CDs partially overlaps with the absorption spectrum of quencher. Energy transfer from CDs to quencher without the appearance of a photon due to the long-range dipole-dipole interaction (Diac et al., 2015). This quenching has the following three characteristics (Liu, Xu, et al., 2017; Zu et al., 2017).
(a)
The fluorescence spectrum of CDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of quencher.
(b)
The distance between CDs and quencher ranges from 0.1 nm to 10 nm (Liang, Kang, Payne, Wang, & Sun, 2016).
(c)
The fluorescence lifetime of CDs will decrease.

Quenching of CDs by MnO2 is a representative of FRET (Fig. 7 a).(Wei et al., 2018) The UV–vis absorption spectra of MnO2 and the emission spectrum of CDs obviously overlap (Fig. 7b) (Yan et al., 2016), and the distance between CDs and large, medium and small sized MnO2 nanospheres is 2.53 nm, 3.88 nm and 5.0 nm, respectively (Fig. 7c). The fluorescence lifetime of CDs is shortened by 27%, 14% and 7%, respectively. It is proved that MnO2 can quench the fluorescence of CDs by FRET, while GSH can reduce MnO2 to Mn2+ and restore the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 7d, e, f).

PET refers to the process of electron transfer between CDs and quencher. The donor and acceptor of electron form cationic free radicals and anionic free radicals, respectively. Complex formed by them can return to the ground state without emitting photons. PET can be divided into reduced PET and oxidized PET. Reduced PET refers to CDs obtains electron as an electron acceptor while oxidized PET is opposite (Fan et al., 2017). PET has the following two characteristics (Zu et al., 2017):
(a)
Fluorescence lifetime of CDs is shortened.
(b)
There is an energy gap of the LUMO and HOMO or the LUMO and LUMO between the CDs and the quencher.

Quenching between some CDs and nitrite demonstrates the process of PET (Li, Huang, et al., 2020). In this report, CDs synthesized with m-phenylenediamine contain primary arylamines, which can be diazotized by NO2− to form diazonium salts in strongly acidic media. The FTIR spectroscopy of CDs after the diazo coupling reaction with NO2− indicated that the aromatic amine peaks at 3340 and 3210 cm−1 were greatly weakened and nearly vanished, while a new peak at 1687 cm−1 appeared which was attributed to the formation of a diazo group. Therefore, a photoinduced electron transfer process from the excited state of the CDs to the azo compounds on their surface.

IFE is a phenomenon that excitation light or emission light of CDs is absorbed by quencher or CDs itself, which usually occurs when there is excessive CDs or quencher in solution (Liu, Xu, et al., 2017). Therefore, IFE has the following two characteristics:
(a)
The excitation or emission spectrum of fluorescent substances overlaps with the absorption spectrum of light-absorbing substances.
(b)
It does not affect the fluorescence attenuation process of CDs itself, and does not change the absorption peak and fluorescence lifetime of CDs.

A typical example of IFE is the detection of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) (Huang et al., 2019). When AChE exists, o-phenylenediamine (OPD) will be continuously catalyzed and oxidized to form fluorescent 2,3- diaminophenazine (DAP). otherwise, a large amount of OPD will accumulate (Fig. 8 a). OPD has no absorption in a wavelength range from 350 to 600 nm, while the absorption spectrum of DAP overlaps with the emission spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 8b). Meanwhile, the addition of DAP does not affect the fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 8c). This proves that DAP can absorb the emission light of N-CDs through IFE, which changes the fluorescence emission of the system to detect AChE.

The fluorescence intensity of most CDs is strong in dilute solution but be greatly weakened at high concentration, which is called ACQ. ACQ is widely present in many fluorescent materials. Due to the planar conjugated structure, excimer or exciplex will be formed through π-π interaction or other non-radiative channels. Therefore, energy tends to be converted between molecules in the form of non-radiative rather than fluorescent emission.

Although ACQ is generally considered harmful, CDs fluorescent probes based on it are common in bacterial detection. Combining with specific groups on the surface of bacteria. For example, the fluorescent CD@Van was obtained by modifying vancomycin on the surface of CDs (Zhong, Zhuo, Feng, & Yang, 2015). As shown in Fig. 9 , the detection of Gram-positive bacteria focuses on the gathered CD@Van on the surface of bacteria by the interaction of ligand-receptors, which leads to the linear decrease of fluorescence intensity.

The process of CDs recovering from the quenched state to the fluorescent state is called fluorescence recovery. Many detected substances cannot directly quench CDs, but they can react with quenchers to eliminate the quenchers’ effect and restore the fluorescence of CDs. In this case, the fluorescence intensity of CDs and the concentration of target substance are positively correlated, and shows a linear relationship within a certain interval.

For example, there is a kind of CDs for caffeine detection by using Cu2+ (Nemati, Hosseini, Zare-Dorabei, Salehnia, & Ganjali, 2018). The fluorescence lifetimes of CDs and “CDs-Cu2+" are 2.30 ns and 1.52 ns respectively, which was a dynamic quenching. Caffeine could induce Cu2+ to dissociate from the surface of CDs and recover the fluorescence, thus realizing the detection of caffeine.

Aggregation-induced luminescence enhancement (AIE) is the opposite of ACQ. The non-radiative energy conversion of some particles at low concentrations is more severe than at high concentrations, resulting in increased fluorescence with increasing concentrations.

AIE molecules are diverse. But compared with small molecules, organic and metal nanomaterials, CDs exhibit optical properties with enhanced stability. Essentially, restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM) is generally considered to be the mechanism of AIE. In dilute solution, due to the active groups on the particle surface, energy can be consumed by molecular vibration, resulting in low fluorescence efficiency. In the aggregate state, due to the restriction of molecular motion, the hindered rotation and vibration of the surface groups on the CDs, reduces the non-radiative decay, facilitating the AIE effect.

AIE can coexist with ACQ, which manifests as a change in emission wavelength with increasing concentration. For example, the interaction between human hemoglobin (HHB) and CDs was evaluated (Chakraborty et al., 2019). As shown in Fig. 10 , a complex was formed between CDs and HHB due to the absorption of tryptophan and tyrosine to CDs. At the same time, the fluorescence spectra of the two CDs showed that not only the fluorescence was enhanced, but significant red shift occurred. In addition, AIE has also been applied to detect metal ions in food.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CDs in food detection

ce:section.ce:para: Risks of food safety exist in the whole process of cultivation, production, processing, storage, transportation, sales and consumption (Fig. 11 ) (Lin et al., 2020). Therefore, residual metal ion, drug, additive and pathogenic bacteria in food are necessary to be detected to ensure safety. For another, the edible value of food can be evaluated by detecting the content of nutrients. By comparing the fluorescence response mechanism when detecting these substances, the relationship between the target substance and the CDs probe is explored.

Metal ions left in food through dust, soil and sewage (Dasari, Cho, Jaćimović, Sun, & Yim, 2020) can lead to serious diseases (Plegaria, Dzul, Zuiderweg, Stemmler, & Pecoraro, 2015). Cr6+ and Pb2+ can cause liver and kidney poisoning (An et al., 2019; Chaleshtari & Foudazi, 2020). Hg2+ can cause Minamata disease (James et al., 2020). Cadmium rice due to Cd2+ residue can cause “Itai-Itai disease” (Yu et al., 2017). Food polluted by industrial wastewater may lead to acute poisoning due to residual Fe3+ (Soares, Puntel, & Avila, 2018). Excessive intake of Al3+ will increase the incidence of Alzheimer's disease (Promyo, Iqbal, Chaidee, & Chetsawang, 2020). Representative detection of metal ions utilizing CDs are listed in Table 1 .

Most harmful metal ions that may be present in food can be detected by CDs. And in most cases, the LOD is lower than the requirements of relevant safety standards. Precursors are abundant, mainly including small molecular organics and biomass materials.

Nitrogen is the most common doping element, and amino groups are often used as nitrogen sources. Pyridinic N, pyrrolic N or graphitic N, etc. may be converted owing to the intermolecular dehydration during synthesis (Zhao, Jin, Zhang, & Peng, 2021). On account of the rich variety and high activity of nitrogen-containing functional groups, N-CDs are more likely to interact with metal ions and combine with coordinate bonds to form stable complexes, which improves their specificity and sensitivity.

The fluorescence response of element-doped CDs are mainly static quenching, while dynamic quenching often appears together with non-doped CDs. This is because the common functional groups of non-doped CDs are hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, whose interaction with metal ions is not specific (Zhu et al., 2013).Therefore, introducing functional groups that can specifically bind to target metal ions through element doping is a more mature idea.

For example, by XPS characterization of N-CDs before and after Fe3+ addition, the peaks of N1s remarkably decreased, while the change of the carbon groups was negligible. This indicated that Fe3+ was connected to the N atom by Fe–N coordinate bonds (Zhao et al., 2021), which were so strong that the PL intensity of N-CDs could be barely turned on in the presence of Fe3+ chelating reagent (Zhang, Li, et al., 2014). In another report, the emission peak of N-CDs red-shift after the addition of Cr6+, which indicated the high selectivity of N-CDs came from the coordination interaction between Cr6+ and imidazole N (Zhang, Jin, Liu, Wang, & Jiao, 2020). AIE caused by the binding force of Cu2+ and amino groups is occasionally used (Lv et al., 2019). In addition to nitrogen, the sulfhydryl of CDs synthesized from cherries tomatoes could interact with Hg2+ and decrease their fluorescence intensity (P. Wang, Wang, et al., 2016). Although the functional groups are different, the strong binding force between metal ions and CDs is always the basis of detection.

There are still some metal ions such as Mn2+, Zn2+, Sn2+, etc., which are rarely reported to be detected. Although them are low-toxic, it can still be harmful if the content is too high. Therefore, CDs with a wider linear detection range are necessary. In addition, most studies take self-made solutions as test objects instead of real food samples, especially grains and soil that are easily polluted by industrial wastewater (Zhang, Tang, et al., 2018).

The residues of pesticides (Yildirim Kumral, Kumral, Kolcu, Maden, & Artik, 2020), fungicides (Sinisi et al., 2019) and herbicides have seriously affected the safety of agricultural products, especially for vegetables (Fan et al., 2013) and fruits (Golge & Kabak, 2018). Appropriate use of pesticides can protect crops (Zhang, Asiri, et al., 2014a), while excessive spraying of pesticides will bring harm to both crops and human. Some typical pesticides detected by CDs are summarized in Table 2 . The detection mechanism and application cases were also reviewed.

Many classic pesticides have been detectable by CDs. Among them, insecticides have been reported the most, while antibacterial agents and herbicides are rarely mentioned. Most linear detection ranges are narrow. most CDs can achieve sufficiently low LOD. Since our purpose of pesticide detection is to confirm whether the content exceeds the standard, LOD is more valuable.

Most CDs are prepared by hydrothermal treatment and elemental doping is not typical. However, most CDs cannot detect pesticides independently. According to current researches, the mechanisms of CDs in pesticide detection mainly includes the following categories, and the principle is shown in Fig. 12 .

Firstly, enzyme-based fluorescence “on-off-on” and “off-on-off” switches, which can be divided into the following two types:
(a)
Fluorescence “on-off-on” caused by enzymatic reaction. During this process, fluorescence of CDs is quenched by the products of enzymatic reaction. Then the concentration of the enzymatic reaction products is reduced by using pesticides as enzyme inhibitors, thus realizing fluorescence recovery (Huang et al., 2019).
(b)
Fluorescence “off-on-off” caused by the combination of enzymatic reaction and quencher. Fluorescence of CDs was quenched by nanoparticles, metal ions or small polymer molecules. After that, the enzymatic reaction product reacts with quencher to recover fluorescence. When pesticides are added, they act as enzyme inhibitors to destroy the enzymatic reaction, which leads to fluorescence quenching again (Chen, Qin, Yuan, Shi, & Wang, 2020).

Secondly, fluorescence “on-off-on” mechanism caused by nanoparticles. CDs are quenched by nanoparticles through FRET or IFE. When the pesticide was added, it directly combines with nanoparticles to restore the fluorescence (Yang, Zhu, Chen, Chen, & Zhou, 2018). There is no enzyme involved in the whole process.

Enzyme-based method relies on pesticides as enzyme inhibitors, which has significant advantages in detecting pesticides with similar structure (such as OPs). By contrast, nanoparticle-based method has a higher specificity. Therefore, the current main idea of designing CDs with pesticide detection function is to select an enzymatic reaction or nanoparticle that can be interfered by the target pesticide. Then, CDs that can be quenched by the selected reaction/nanoparticles are synthesized. This method is complicated and direct fluorescence quenching or enhancement may be a better choice. It can be found from limited reports that these CDs are characterized by abundant functional groups on the surface.

Drugs that can be used to regulate animal physiological function can be called veterinary drugs. Veterinary drugs are easily left in meat, eggs, and dairy products, which are harmful to human health (Zhou, Zou, Song, & Chen, 2018). A subset of the veterinary drugs tested with CDs are listed in Table 3 .

CDs has been used to quantitatively detect veterinary drugs in many real food samples, showing the advantages of high recovery rate and low LOD. However, there is usually more than one veterinary drug residual in food. CDs with the ability to detect multiple veterinary drugs is still rare.
In the process of preparation, small molecular organic matter is the main carbon source and many other elements are introduced to obtain abundant carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino and sulfhydryl group.

The fluorescence change “on-off-on” detection mechanism combined with metal ions or nanoparticles plays an important role in veterinary drug detection (Fig. 13 ). For example, N-CDs can be quenched by Cu2+, and ciprofloxacin can combine with Cu2+ to separate it from CDs and realize fluorescence recovery (Dang, Ganganboina, & Doong, 2020).

There are also reports of direct fluorescence quenching or enhancement (Mishra, Pulidindi, Kabha, & Gedanken, 2016). But in most cases, CDs cannot directly combine with veterinary drugs, which may be due to the lack of functional groups that can interact with them. Therefore, fluorescence recovery is a more mature and regular method to detect veterinary drugs. After a particle that is easy to combine with target veterinary drug was found, it is easier to synthesize CDs that can be quenched by it. Among them, metal ions are more recommended because they are cheaper than nanoparticles such as WS2 and AuNPs.

Food additives can improve the color, smell and taste of food, but the excessive addition of them will endanger people's health (Chazelas et al., 2020). Some illegal additives can result in serious food safety problems. Table 4 lists some representative food additives and their detection mechanism.

Food additives that can be detected by CDs are mainly artificial colors and preservatives, which could be detected by different CDs in multiple forms. However, many illegal food additives such as sodium formaldehyde sulfoxylate, fluorescent whitening agent, plasticizer, etc. are lacking in research. The detection of some artificial sweeteners such as saccharin, cyclamate, aspartame, etc. is also rare. Compared with developing new CDs for the detection of pigments and preservatives, it is more practical to study these food additives that have received less attention before.
The synthesis methods of CDs are diverse and elemental doping is occasionally used. The detection samples are abundant and low enough LOD can be obtained in real food.

The detection mechanisms for food additives are diverse due to the wide variety and sundry molecular structures. Most of them can still be attributed to direct fluorescence quenching. FRET and static quenching are common, while dynamic quenching, PET and IFE are rare. CDs probes are confirmed to be designed based on the molecular characteristics of target additives, because most additives have unique functional groups such as azo, sulfonic and NO2 −. This can be confirmed from the fluorescence response mechanism since FRET and static quenching are both dependent on functional groups. For example, CDs synthesized with Citric acid and polyethylene glycol can emit bright blue fluorescence. Carboxyl on its surface can form hydrogen bond with sulfonic group of carmine to form a stable complex, resulting in static quenching (Zhang, Na, Jiang, Lou, & Jin, 2016). In particular, metal ions and nanoparticles are frequently used as an aid to detect melamine.

Many pathogens can easily enter human body through food, causing a series of diseases (Mondal, N, Ramlal, & Kingston, 2018; Shi et al., 2018; Yan, O Brien, Shelton, Whelen, & Pagaling, 2018). Table 5 lists the detection methods of some common food-borne pathogens. The detection mechanism, restraint and practical application cases are summarized.

Most traditional pathogenic bacteria can be detected by CDs with good sensitivity and ideal linear detection range. The fluorescence intensity can be increased at least two orders of magnitude by encapsulating CDs in organic silica nano-capsules, thus reducing the detection limit (Yang, et al., 2018). Interestingly, some CDs have been confirmed not only to detect bacteria, but also have significant antibacterial activity, which are promising alternative to antibiotics.

However, most CDs can only distinguish Gram-negative bacteria from Gram-positive bacteria. The specific type of bacteria cannot be accurately identified. In addition, few researches have focused on the detection of viruses. However, many viruses can exist in food and drinking water (Usman, Farooq, & Hanna, 2020), threatening people's health. It is an extremely valuable field at present.

The detection mechanism of pathogenic bacteria with CDs can all be summarized as molecular aggregation, including AIE, ACQ and static quenching. Therefore, the focus of detection is to combine CDs with the characteristic groups on the bacteria. But most CDs have poor binding ability with bacteria. Therefore, an inducing molecular that can not only bind to both the target bacteria and CDs is necessary, which determines the sensitivity and specificity of CDs in most cases. Antigen-antibody biosensors are recognized as effective. If multiple bacteria need to be detected at the same time, then broad-spectrum antibiotics, such as vancomycin, are recommended (Zhong et al., 2015). If you want to achieve specific detection, dedicated aptamers should be chosen or designed (Fig. 14 ) (Wang, Xu, et al., 2015).
adopted with permission from (Wang, Han, et al., 2015). Copy rights of Royal society of chemistry.

Nutrients in food mainly include carbohydrates (Rossini, Milani, & Pezza, 2019), vitamins (Bito et al., 2016; Han, Zou, Gao, & Huang, 2016), amino acids, polypeptides/proteins (Hua et al., 2019; Song et al., 2014) and various special functional substances. Table 6 shows the detection of some representative nutrients and functional components, and summarizes detection mechanism and practical application cases.

Current research has included most common nutrients and functional ingredients. Sufficiently low LOD and strong anti-interference capabilities have been proven in many real food samples. Different from harmful substances, the focus of nutrients detection should be content determination rather than simple judgment of presence or absence, and the upper limit of detection is more valuable than LOD. But the linear detection range of most CDs is not wide enough. For example, the content of vitamin C in strawberries can exceed 40 mg/100 mL, which is nearly three times the current maximum detection limit (Odriozola-Serrano, Soliva-Fortuny, Gimeno-Añó, & Martín-Belloso, 2008). Therefore, CDs can be used to determine the presence of nutrients in processed foods. But it is not a good method for evaluating the nutritional value of food currently.

Doped elements and nanoparticles are frequently used to prepare CDs probes, while ordinary CDs are rare. But unlike the interaction with metal ions, PET and FRET are the most common ways to detect nutrients rather than static quenching. This is because the common functional groups on the surface of CDs, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, etc., are also widely present in nutrients, which makes CDs unable to specifically bind to the target. Therefore, the purpose of element doping is mainly to adjust the excitation spectrum and polarity of CDs, make energy or electron transfer between CDs and target substances easier. For example, as an acidic amino acid, aspartate is used as an electron-withdrawing group to attract free electrons of N-CDs and cause fluorescence quenching (Tabaraki & Abdi, 2019).

When hydroxyl or carboxyl groups are abundant in target substance, it is difficult to detect with CDs alone. At this time, fluorescence recovery using metal ions or nanoparticles is the main method. Generally, metal ions in the system were used to quench CDs first. Then the target molecule reacted with metal ions to recover the fluorescence (Wang, Liu, Zhang, & Lv, 2012). In particular, during the detection of tea polyphenol, the quenching of CDs with Cu2+ was caused by reactive oxygen radicals generated from the interaction between tea polyphenol and Cu2+ (Wei, Liu, et al., 2020; Zheng et al., 2006).

Comprehensive detection of nutrients is difficult to achieve due to their wide variety. As a method with convenience as the main advantage, more attention should be paid to the detection of common and necessary nutrients for human body.

Food detection probe is a widely discussed and promising application of CDs. Hydrothermal treatment is the typical synthesis method and most CDs are water-soluble. Small-molecule organics and biomass materials are more and more frequently selected as precursors. Lower detection limit has become the goal of researchers.

The detection mechanism of CDs depends on the nature of target substance. The basis of metal ion detection is their coordination with the functional groups of CDs. In the detection of pesticides and veterinary drugs, fluorescent switches based on enzymes or nanoparticles is the mainstream. Food additives usually cause abundant fluorescence quenching. For the detection of bacteria, the main method is to induce the aggregation of CDs. Since many nutrients have functional groups similar to CDs, the detection for them is based on energy or electron transfer, and metal ion-based fluorescent switch control is also common.

It can be found that the interaction between CDs and target substances is the focus of fluorescent probe design. This interaction can be the reaction, polymerization, electron transfer between molecules, or the overlap of excitation/emission spectra, which can be adjusted and strengthened by element doping. The choice of reaction mechanism is the prerequisite for designing the element composition and surface state of CDs probes, and it is also the basis for selecting precursors and synthesis methods. If these phenomena cannot occur directly between CDs and the target, metal ions or nanoparticles that can interact with both of them need to be added. In addition, it is also an effective method to introduce a specific reaction of the target substance first, and then detect the reaction product through CDs. Most CDs probes are included in the above three types, which can be used as a general design route (Fig. 15 ).

There are also some problems in the current research. The reports about low-toxic metal ions, sweeteners, brighteners, plasticizers, thickeners and food-borne viruses are rare. Wide linear detection range has been overlooked, which is more valuable in quantitative detection. The immunity of CDs to pH changes needs to be further improved. More applications other than liquid reagents should be explored to maximize the advantages of CDs in rapid detection, such as test strips and portable fluorescent identification devices.
